1.1: This is the family record of Jesus the Messiah, a descendant of David and Abraham.
1.2: Abraham was the father of Isaac, Isaac of Jacob, and Jacob of Judah and his brothers.
1.3: Judah fathered Perez and Zerah through Tamar; Perez fathered Hezron; Hezron fathered Ram.
1.4: Ram fathered Amminadab; Amminadab fathered Nahshon; Nahshon fathered Salmon.
1.5: Salmon fathered Boaz through Rahab; Boaz fathered Obed through Ruth; Obed fathered Jesse.
1.6: Jesse fathered King David. David fathered Solomon, whose mother had been Uriah's wife.
1.7: Solomon fathered Rehoboam; Rehoboam fathered Abijah; Abijah fathered Asa.
1.8: Asa fathered Jehoshaphat; Jehoshaphat fathered Joram; Joram fathered Uzziah.
1.9: Uzziah fathered Jotham; Jotham fathered Ahaz; Ahaz fathered Hezekiah.
1.10: Hezekiah fathered Manasseh; Manasseh fathered Amos; Amos fathered Josiah.
1.11: Josiah fathered Jechoniah and his brothers at the time of the Babylonian exile.
1.12: After the Babylonian exile: Jechoniah fathered Shealtiel; Shealtiel fathered Zerubbabel.
1.13: Zerubbabel fathered Abiud; Abiud fathered Eliakim; Eliakim fathered Azor.
1.14: Azor fathered Zadok; Zadok fathered Achim; Achim fathered Eliud.
1.15: Eliud fathered Eleazar; Eleazar fathered Matthan; Matthan fathered Jacob.
1.16: Jacob fathered Joseph, the husband of Mary. Mary gave birth to Jesus, who is called the Messiah.
1.17: In all, from Abraham to David is fourteen generations; from David to the Babylonian exile, fourteen more; and from the exile to the Messiah, fourteen more.
1.18: The birth of Jesus the Messiah took place in this way: his mother Mary was engaged to Joseph, but before they were married, it was discovered that she was pregnant—through the Holy Spirit.
1.19: Joseph, to whom she was engaged, was an honorable man. Rather than humiliate her publicly, he planned to break off the engagement privately.
1.20: As Joseph was thinking all this through, God's angel appeared to him in a dream and said: Joseph, heir of David, don't be afraid to marry Mary—the child she carries was conceived by the Holy Spirit.
1.21: She will have a son, and you are to name him Jesus—because he will rescue his people from the power of sin.
1.22: All of this happened to bring to completion what God had spoken through the prophet:
1.23: The virgin will become pregnant and give birth to a son, and they will name him Immanuel—a name that means God is with us.
1.24: Joseph woke from sleep and did exactly what God's angel had told him to do—he took Mary as his wife.
1.25: He did not sleep with her until she had given birth to her son. And he named the child Jesus.

2.1: Jesus was born in Bethlehem in the Judean territory, during the reign of King Herod. Not long after, astrologers from the east arrived in Jerusalem
2.2: and asked: Where is the newborn King of the Jews? We saw his star rise and have traveled here to honor him.
2.3: News of this threw King Herod into alarm, and the whole city of Jerusalem along with him.
2.4: He called together all the chief priests and religious scholars and demanded to know where the Messiah was supposed to be born.
2.5: In Bethlehem in Judea, they answered, because that is what the prophet recorded:
2.6: Bethlehem in the land of Judah, you are far from insignificant among the leading cities of Judah—for a ruler will emerge from you who will be the shepherd-king of my people Israel.
2.7: Herod then secretly summoned the astrologers and questioned them carefully about the precise time the star had appeared.
2.8: He sent them on to Bethlehem with these instructions: Go and make a thorough search for the child. When you find him, come back and tell me—I want to come and honor him myself.
2.9: They set out as the king instructed. And the star they had seen rise reappeared, leading them forward until it stopped and hovered over the exact place where the child was.
2.10: The sight of the star filled them with overwhelming joy.
2.11: They entered the house and saw the child with Mary his mother. They fell to the ground and worshipped him, then opened their gift-boxes and presented him with gold, fragrant incense, and myrrh.
2.12: They had been warned in a dream not to report back to Herod, so they returned home by a different road.
2.13: After the astrologers had left, God's angel appeared to Joseph in a dream: Get up now—take the child and his mother and flee to Egypt. Stay there until I give you word, because Herod is hunting for the child to have him killed.
2.14: Joseph got up that same night, took the child and his mother, and set off for Egypt.
2.15: They stayed there until Herod died. This brought to fulfillment what God had spoken through the prophet: Out of Egypt I summoned my son.
2.16: When Herod realized the astrologers had outmaneuvered him, his rage was volcanic. He ordered the slaughter of every male child in Bethlehem and the surrounding region who was two years old or younger—using the date of the star's appearance that he had so carefully extracted from the astrologers.
2.17: This fulfilled the words spoken through the prophet Jeremiah:
2.18: A voice echoed from Ramah—weeping, bitter anguish. Rachel weeping for her children, refusing any comfort, because they were gone.
2.19: When Herod finally died, God's angel appeared to Joseph in a dream while he was still in Egypt.
2.20: The angel said: Get up, take the child and his mother back to the land of Israel—the men who wanted the child dead are themselves now dead.
2.21: Joseph got up, took the child and his mother, and returned to the land of Israel.
2.22: But when he learned that Archelaus had taken his father Herod's place as ruler over Judea, Joseph was afraid to go there. Directed by another dream, he rerouted to the region of Galilee instead.
2.23: He settled in a town called Nazareth—bringing to fulfillment what the prophets had said, that he would be called a Nazarene.

3.1: In that period, John the Baptizer appeared, preaching in the Judean wilderness.
3.2: His message was direct: Turn back to God—the heavenly reign is arriving now.
3.3: Isaiah the prophet had described him centuries earlier: A voice calling out in the desert—Clear the road for the Lord, straighten the path before him!
3.4: John's clothing was camel-hair fabric with a leather belt at his waist. He ate locusts and wild honey.
3.5: People streamed out to him from Jerusalem, from all over Judea, and from the whole Jordan valley.
3.6: They confessed their sins and were baptized by him in the Jordan.
3.7: When John saw a crowd of Pharisees and Sadducees arriving for baptism, he confronted them: You snake-spawn! Who tipped you off to run from the coming judgment?
3.8: Show by the way you live that you've genuinely turned around.
3.9: Don't console yourselves by saying: But we're Abraham's descendants. I'm telling you, God could turn these stones into Abraham's children if he wanted.
3.10: The axe is already positioned at the base of the trees. Every tree that fails to produce good fruit will be chopped down and tossed into the fire.
3.11: I baptize you with water as a sign of turning to God. But the one coming after me is far more powerful than I am—I'm not even worthy to carry his sandals. He will baptize you with the Holy Spirit and with fire.
3.12: He comes with his threshing fork ready. He will separate the grain from the husks, storing the wheat and burning the chaff in a fire that cannot be put out.
3.13: At that time Jesus came from Galilee to the Jordan River to be baptized by John.
3.14: John tried to stop him: You should be baptizing me—why are you coming to me?
3.15: Jesus answered: Let it happen now—this is the right thing to do to bring all that God requires to completion. John agreed.
3.16: As Jesus rose from the water, the sky suddenly opened and he saw God's Spirit descending on him in the form of a dove.
3.17: And a voice from heaven declared: This is my Son, the one I cherish—he brings me great joy.

4.1: The Spirit then led Jesus out into the wilderness to be tested by the devil.
4.2: After forty days and nights without food, he was famished.
4.3: The tempter approached and said: If you really are the Son of God, order these rocks to turn into bread.
4.4: Jesus replied: Scripture says, Human beings don't live on food alone—they live on every word that comes from God.
4.5: Next the devil transported him to the holy city of Jerusalem and positioned him on the highest point of the temple.
4.6: The devil said: If you truly are the Son of God, jump off—because Scripture says God will order his angels to catch you, and they'll hold you up so you don't even stub your toe on a stone.
4.7: Jesus responded: But Scripture also says, Don't put God to a foolish test.
4.8: For the final test, the devil took him to the summit of a towering mountain and displayed before him every kingdom in the world in all its splendour.
4.9: I'll give all of this to you, the devil said, if you'll simply kneel down and worship me.
4.10: Jesus replied sharply: Get away from me, Satan! Scripture says, Worship and serve God alone.
4.11: The devil left, and angels came and cared for him.
4.12: When Jesus learned that John had been arrested, he pulled back to Galilee.
4.13: He left Nazareth and settled in Capernaum, a lakeside town in the territory of Zebulun and Naphtali.
4.14: This fulfilled what Isaiah the prophet had written:
4.15: The territory of Zebulun and Naphtali, along the Sea road, east of the Jordan—Galilee of the nations—
4.16: the people who sat in deep darkness have seen a blazing light; on those entombed in the region of death's shadow, light has burst forth.
4.17: From that point on, Jesus began proclaiming the same message: Turn back to God—the heavenly reign is arriving now.
4.18: As he walked along the shore of the Sea of Galilee, Jesus spotted two brothers—Simon, known as Peter, and his brother Andrew—throwing a fishing net into the water.
4.19: Jesus called out to them: Come, follow me, and I'll make you fishers of people.
4.20: Without hesitation they abandoned their nets and went with him.
4.21: A little further along the shore he saw another pair of brothers—James and John, the sons of Zebedee—sitting in their boat with their father, repairing their nets. Jesus called to them.
4.22: They immediately left the boat and their father and followed him.
4.23: Jesus traveled all through Galilee, teaching in the synagogues, announcing the good news of God's reign, and healing every kind of illness and disability among the people.
4.24: Word about him spread throughout Syria, and people started bringing him all their sick—those suffering from painful diseases, the demon-possessed, those with seizure disorders, and paralyzed people—and he healed every one of them.
4.25: Enormous crowds began following him from Galilee, the Ten-City region, Jerusalem, Judea, and from the far side of the Jordan.

5.1: Seeing the crowds gathering, Jesus climbed the hillside and sat down. His disciples gathered around him,
5.2: and he began to teach them.
5.3: Those who know their spiritual poverty are favored by God—the heavenly kingdom belongs to them.
5.4: Those who grieve and weep are favored by God—they will receive deep comfort.
5.5: Those who are gentle and humble are favored by God—they will inherit the whole earth.
5.6: Those who are hungry and thirsty for what is right are favored by God—their longing will be satisfied.
5.7: Those who show compassion are favored by God—they will receive compassion in return.
5.8: Those with undivided, sincere hearts are favored by God—they will see God himself.
5.9: Those who actively make peace are favored by God—they will be recognized as his children.
5.10: Those who face opposition for doing what is right are favored by God—the heavenly kingdom belongs to them.
5.11: You are favored by God when people insult you, chase you off, and lie about you because of your connection to me.
5.12: Celebrate—jump for joy! Your reward in heaven is enormous. Remember, the prophets before you were treated just the same way.
5.13: You are the earth's salt. But if salt goes flat, what can possibly make it salty again? It becomes useless—only fit to be thrown out and stepped on.
5.14: You are the world's light. A city on a hilltop can't be concealed.
5.15: Nobody lights a lamp and covers it with a bucket—they put it on a stand where it lights the whole room.
5.16: In the same way, let your light blaze before people so they can see the good you do and praise your Father in heaven.
5.17: Don't assume I've come to tear down the Law and the Prophets—I came not to demolish them but to bring them to complete fulfilment.
5.18: I'm telling you plainly: not the smallest letter or punctuation mark in the Law will disappear before heaven and earth are gone and everything it points to has come to pass.
5.19: So whoever dismisses even the smallest of these commands—and teaches others to do the same—will hold the lowest position in God's kingdom. But those who keep them and teach them will hold the highest.
5.20: Let me be direct with you: unless the way you live is far more genuinely righteous than that of the Pharisees and religious scholars, you will not enter God's kingdom at all.
5.21: You've been taught the ancient command: Don't commit murder; and murderers must face the court.
5.22: But I'm going further: anyone who nurses resentment against a fellow human is already in danger of judgment. Calling someone worthless makes you answerable before the religious council. And calling someone a fool puts you in danger of the fire of Gehenna.
5.23: So if you're bringing your offering to the altar and you suddenly remember that someone has a grievance against you,
5.24: leave your offering right there at the altar and go make things right with that person first. Then come back and give your offering.
5.25: Settle things fast with anyone who has a legal case against you while you're still on the way to court—otherwise they'll hand you to the judge, who'll hand you to the bailiff, and you'll end up in prison.
5.26: I promise you, you won't get out until you've paid every last cent.
5.27: You know the commandment: Don't commit adultery.
5.28: But I'm taking it further: anyone who deliberately looks at a woman with sexual desire has already committed adultery with her at the level of the heart.
5.29: If your right eye is leading you into sin, gouge it out and throw it away—it's far better to lose one body part than for your whole self to end up in Gehenna.
5.30: If your right hand is leading you into sin, cut it off and throw it away—better to lose a hand than for your whole self to end up in Gehenna.
5.31: The old rule was: Anyone who sends his wife away must give her a proper divorce document.
5.32: But I say this: anyone who divorces his wife—unless she has been sexually unfaithful—exposes her to adultery. And anyone who marries a woman so divorced commits adultery.
5.33: You've also heard the ancient teaching: Don't break your oaths—follow through on whatever you've vowed to God.
5.34: But my instruction is this: don't take oaths at all—not by heaven, which is God's throne;
5.35: not by the earth, which is his footstool; not by Jerusalem, which is the great King's city.
5.36: Don't even swear by your own head—you can't make a single hair white or black.
5.37: Just say yes when you mean yes, and no when you mean no. Anything more elaborate comes from the deceiver.
5.38: You know the principle: An eye for an eye, a tooth for a tooth.
5.39: But I'm overturning that: don't meet force with force. If someone strikes you on the right cheek, offer the other one as well.
5.40: If someone drags you to court to take your shirt, give them your coat too.
5.41: If a soldier commands you to carry his pack a mile, carry it two.
5.42: Give to those who ask, and don't turn away those who want to borrow from you.
5.43: You've heard the teaching: Love your neighbor—and hate your enemy.
5.44: But my instruction is radically different: Love your enemies. Pray for the people who target you.
5.45: This is how you will show you are children of your Father in heaven—because he lets the sun shine on both good and evil people, and sends rain on both the righteous and unrighteous alike.
5.46: What's so remarkable about loving people who already love you? Even tax collectors do that much.
5.47: And if you're friendly only to your own circle, what sets you apart? Even outsiders do that.
5.48: Grow into the complete goodness of your heavenly Father.

6.1: Watch out—don't put your acts of devotion on display for others to admire. If you do, you earn nothing from your Father in heaven.
6.2: So when you give to those in need, don't make a fanfare of it the way pretenders do in the synagogues and on the street corners—performing for public admiration. That admiration is all the reward they're ever going to get.
6.3: But when you give, do it so privately that your left hand doesn't know what your right hand is doing.
6.4: Keep your generosity hidden, and your Father who observes what is hidden will reward you.
6.5: And when you pray, don't perform the way pretenders do—they love to stand praying in synagogues and on street corners where everyone can see them. That public attention is all they're going to get.
6.6: Instead, go to a private place, close the door, and pray to your Father who is there in the hidden place. Your Father who sees the hidden will reward you.
6.7: When you pray, don't pile up words the way pagans do—they think the more they say, the better the chance God will hear them.
6.8: Don't imitate them. Your Father already knows what you need before you ask.
6.9: This is the pattern for your prayer: Our Father in heaven—may your name be held holy,
6.10: may your reign arrive, your will be done on earth just as it is in heaven.
6.12: Forgive us our wrongs, as we have forgiven those who have wronged us.
6.13: And keep us from being pulled toward wrongdoing; rescue us from the evil one. For the kingdom, the power, and the glory belong to you forever. Amen.
6.14: Because if you forgive others for what they've done against you, your heavenly Father will forgive you too.
6.15: But if you refuse to forgive others, your Father will not forgive what you've done either.
6.16: When you fast, don't make yourself look miserable the way pretenders do—they make their faces haggard so everyone will know they're fasting. That public recognition is their full reward.
6.17: Instead, when you fast, wash your face and fix your hair as usual.
6.18: Then your fast is between you and your Father in the hidden place—not a performance for others. And your Father who sees what is hidden will reward you.
6.19: Stop stockpiling wealth for yourself here on earth, where moths eat it, rust corrodes it, and thieves can break in and take it.
6.20: Instead, build up wealth in heaven, where moths can't touch it, rust can't erode it, and no thief can reach it.
6.21: Your heart will always follow your treasure.
6.22: The eye is the body's lamp. If your vision is clear and focused, your whole inner life will be bright.
6.23: But if your vision is distorted or corrupt, your whole inner life will be darkened. If what you call light is actually darkness, what profound darkness that must be.
6.24: You can't serve two masters at once—you'll end up loving one and resenting the other, or loyal to one and contemptuous of the other. You simply cannot divide your allegiance between God and Wealth.
6.25: So I'm telling you: stop being anxious about the basics of survival—food and drink and clothing. Isn't there more to life than what you eat? Isn't your body about more than what you wear?
6.26: Watch the birds—they don't plant, harvest, or store grain in warehouses, yet your heavenly Father feeds them all. And you matter far more to God than birds do.
6.27: Besides, which of you has ever extended his life by one hour through worrying?
6.28: And why fret over clothing? Look carefully at the wildflowers—they don't work or weave.
6.29: Yet I'm telling you, even Solomon in the peak of his glory was never dressed as beautifully as one of them.
6.30: If God dresses the grass of the field that way—and it's here today and burnt up tomorrow—don't you think he'll certainly clothe you? How little you trust him!
6.31: So stop asking: What will we eat? What will we drink? What will we wear?
6.32: These are the things that dominate the lives of those who don't know God. But your heavenly Father already knows you need all of them.
6.33: Instead, make God's reign and his way of living your top priority—and all these other things will be provided for you.
6.34: So don't borrow tomorrow's worries today. Tomorrow will bring its own challenges. Today's challenges are enough to deal with.

7.1: Stop sitting in judgment over others, or you will find yourself judged.
7.2: The standard you apply to others will be applied right back to you—the measuring stick you use on others is the one that will be used on you.
7.3: Why are you so focused on the tiny speck in your friend's eye when you have a beam of timber in your own?
7.4: How dare you say to your friend: Here, let me remove that speck from your eye—while you have a plank in your own?
7.5: You are the hypocrite here. Deal with the plank in your own eye first—then you'll see clearly enough to help with the speck in your friend's.
7.6: Don't offer sacred things to those who will only despise them. Don't throw your pearls in front of pigs—they'll just trample them and then turn and attack you.
7.7: Ask and you will receive. Search and you will find. Knock and the door will open.
7.8: Everyone who asks receives; everyone who searches finds; and for everyone who knocks, the door is opened.
7.9: Think about it—is there a parent among you who, when their child asks for bread, would hand them a stone?
7.10: Or when they ask for fish, give them a snake?
7.11: If you flawed, broken people know how to give your children what's good for them, imagine how much more your Father in heaven will give good things to those who ask him.
7.12: Here is the principle that holds everything together: treat others the way you want them to treat you. This is the heart of all Scripture.
7.13: Go in through the narrow gate. The wide gate with the easy road leads to destruction—and crowds of people take that route.
7.14: But the gate that leads to real life is narrow and the path demanding—and only a few ever find it.
7.15: Watch out for fraudulent prophets. They come looking perfectly respectable, but underneath they are predators.
7.16: Their track record will expose them. You don't harvest grapes from thorn-bushes or figs from thistles, do you?
7.17: A healthy tree produces good fruit; a diseased tree produces bad fruit.
7.18: A healthy tree is incapable of producing bad fruit, just as a diseased tree can't produce good fruit.
7.19: Every tree that fails to produce good fruit gets cut down and thrown into the fire.
7.20: So yes—their track record is how you'll recognize them.
7.21: Not everyone who calls out to me Lord, Lord will enter God's kingdom—only those who actually do what my Father in heaven requires.
7.22: On the day of judgment many people will say to me: But Lord, we prophesied in your name! We drove out demons in your name! We performed all kinds of miracles in your name!
7.23: And I will say clearly: I never had a relationship with you. Get away from me—you who lived lawlessly.
7.24: So everyone who hears what I've said here and puts it into practice is like a wise builder who laid his foundation on solid rock.
7.25: The rains poured down, the floodwaters rose, and the storms battered that house—but it didn't collapse because its foundation was on solid rock.
7.26: But everyone who hears what I've said and doesn't act on it is like a foolish builder who laid his foundation on sand.
7.27: When the rains poured, the floods rose, and the storms hit that house, it collapsed—completely and catastrophically.
7.28: When Jesus finished this discourse, the crowds were stunned by his teaching.
7.29: He spoke with genuine authority—nothing like the religious scholars they were used to.

8.1: As he came down the hillside, great crowds followed him.
8.2: A man with leprosy suddenly approached and knelt before him: Lord, if you're willing, you have the power to make me clean.
8.3: Jesus reached out and touched him. I am willing, he said. Be clean. And instantly the leprosy was gone.
8.4: Jesus instructed him firmly: Don't tell anyone about this. Instead, go straight to the priest and bring the offering Moses required—that will be your public verification.
8.5: When Jesus arrived in Capernaum, a Roman centurion approached him with an urgent request.
8.6: Lord, my servant is lying at home paralyzed and in terrible pain.
8.7: Jesus said: I will come and heal him.
8.8: The centurion replied: Lord, I don't deserve to have you enter my house. Just speak the word from here, and my servant will be healed.
8.9: I understand how authority works—I have soldiers under my command. I tell one man to go and he goes; I call another and he comes; I tell my servant to do something and it gets done.
8.10: Jesus was astonished. He turned to the crowd behind him: I'm telling you plainly—nowhere in Israel have I encountered faith like this.
8.11: Many people will come from east and west to take their places at the banquet alongside Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob in God's kingdom.
8.12: But those who assumed the kingdom was theirs by birthright will be thrown out into the outer darkness—where there is weeping and grinding of teeth.
8.13: Jesus turned to the centurion: Go home. What you believed will happen. And the servant was healed at that very hour.
8.14: When Jesus arrived at Peter's home, Peter's mother-in-law was in bed burning with fever.
8.15: He touched her hand and the fever vanished. She got up and began serving them.
8.16: That evening people brought him many who were demon-possessed, and he drove out the evil spirits with a single command, healing everyone who was sick.
8.17: This fulfilled Isaiah's prophecy: He himself took on our weaknesses and carried our diseases.
8.18: Seeing the large crowd surrounding him, Jesus gave the order to cross to the other shore.
8.19: A religious scholar came to him: Teacher, I'll follow you wherever you go.
8.20: Jesus replied: Foxes have their dens; birds have their nests. But the Son of Man has no place to call home.
8.21: Another disciple said: Lord, let me first go and bury my father.
8.22: Jesus replied: Follow me, and let the spiritually dead bury their own dead.
8.23: He got into the boat, and his disciples followed.
8.24: Suddenly a violent storm struck the lake, and the waves were crashing over the boat. Jesus was asleep.
8.25: The disciples rushed to wake him: Lord, save us! We're going under!
8.26: He said: Why are you so terrified? How little you trust me! He stood up and commanded the wind and the waves—and there was a dead calm.
8.27: The disciples were astonished: What kind of person is this—even the wind and waves do what he says?
8.28: On the other side, in the region of the Gadarenes, two men living among the tombs—possessed by demons—came charging toward him. They were so violent that people had stopped using that road.
8.29: They screamed: What business do you have with us, Son of God? Have you come to torture us before the appointed time?
8.30: A large herd of pigs was grazing some distance away.
8.31: The demons pleaded: If you cast us out, send us into that herd of pigs.
8.32: He said: Go. They came out and entered the pigs, and the entire herd stampeded off the cliff into the lake and drowned.
8.33: The herdsmen ran off into town and reported everything—including what had happened to the two demon-possessed men.
8.34: The whole town turned out to meet Jesus—and when they saw him, they begged him to leave their territory.

9.1: Jesus got back in the boat, crossed the lake, and returned to his hometown.
9.2: Some people brought him a paralyzed man on a stretcher. Seeing their faith, Jesus said to the man: Courage, son—your sins are forgiven.
9.3: Some of the religious scholars thought to themselves: This man is blaspheming.
9.4: Jesus knew exactly what they were thinking: Why do you harbor such evil thoughts?
9.5: Which is easier—to say your sins are forgiven, or to say get up and walk?
9.6: So that you'll know the Son of Man has full authority on earth to forgive sins— he turned to the paralyzed man: Get up, pick up your mat, and go home.
9.7: The man got up and walked home.
9.8: The crowds were filled with awe and praised God for giving such authority to human beings.
9.9: As Jesus moved on from there, he spotted a man named Matthew sitting at the tax collection booth. Follow me, Jesus said. Matthew stood up and followed him.
9.10: Later Jesus was dining at Matthew's house, and a crowd of tax collectors and other notorious sinners joined him and his disciples at the table.
9.11: The Pharisees saw this and challenged his disciples: Why does your teacher eat with tax collectors and sinners?
9.12: Jesus overheard them: Healthy people don't need a doctor—sick people do.
9.13: Go and figure out what the Scripture means: I want compassion, not religious performance. I didn't come to call the self-righteous—I came for sinners.
9.14: John's disciples came to Jesus with a question: Why is it that we and the Pharisees fast regularly, but your disciples don't?
9.15: Jesus replied: Should wedding guests be somber while the groom is still at the party? The day will come when the groom is taken away—then they'll fast.
9.16: Nobody patches an old coat with unshrunk cloth—when it shrinks, it pulls away and makes the tear worse.
9.17: And nobody pours new wine into old wineskins—the old skins can't handle the pressure, they burst, and you lose both the wine and the skins. New wine goes in new wineskins, and both are preserved.
9.18: While he was still speaking, a synagogue official came and knelt before him: My daughter has just died. But come and lay your hand on her and she will live.
9.19: Jesus stood up and went with him, along with his disciples.
9.20: On the way, a woman who had been hemorrhaging for twelve years came up behind him and touched the fringe of his garment.
9.21: She had been telling herself: If I can just touch his clothing, I'll be healed.
9.22: Jesus turned and saw her: Take courage, daughter—your faith has healed you. And from that moment the woman was completely well.
9.23: When Jesus arrived at the official's house, he saw the flute players and the crowd in an uproar.
9.24: Step back, he said. The girl is not dead—she's sleeping. They laughed at him.
9.25: Once the crowd was cleared out, he went in, took her hand, and the girl got up.
9.26: Word of this spread through the whole region.
9.27: As Jesus moved on, two blind men followed him, calling out: Have mercy on us, Son of David!
9.28: When he entered the house, the blind men found him there. He asked them: Do you believe I can do this? Yes, Lord, they said.
9.29: He touched their eyes: Let it happen to you according to your faith.
9.30: Their sight was restored. Jesus gave them a strict warning: Don't let anyone know about this.
9.31: But they went out and spread the news all through that area.
9.32: As the blind men were leaving, a man who was mute—possessed by a demon—was brought to Jesus.
9.33: Jesus drove out the demon and the man spoke. The crowds were astonished: Nothing like this has ever been seen in Israel.
9.34: But the Pharisees said: He drives out demons by the power of the chief demon.
9.35: Jesus went through all the towns and villages teaching in their synagogues, proclaiming the good news of the kingdom, and healing every kind of disease and sickness.
9.36: When he looked out at the crowds, his heart broke for them—they were bewildered and abandoned, like sheep with no shepherd.
9.37: He said to his disciples: There is so much harvest waiting, but so few workers.
9.38: So pray to the Lord of the harvest to send workers out into his fields.

10.1: Jesus summoned his twelve disciples and gave them authority to drive out evil spirits and to heal every kind of disease and sickness.
10.2: Here are the names of the twelve apostles: first, Simon (also called Peter) and his brother Andrew; James son of Zebedee and his brother John;
10.3: Philip and Bartholomew; Thomas and Matthew the tax collector; James son of Alphaeus, and Thaddaeus;
10.4: Simon the Zealot and Judas Iscariot, who later betrayed him.
10.5: Jesus sent out these twelve with the following instructions: Stay away from Gentile territory and Samaritan towns.
10.6: Go instead to the lost sheep of Israel.
10.7: As you go, announce this: The heavenly kingdom has arrived.
10.8: Heal the sick, raise the dead, cleanse those with skin diseases, drive out demons. You received freely—give freely.
10.9: Take no gold, silver, or copper money in your belt—
10.10: no travel bag, no extra shirt, no sandals, no walking stick. Workers deserve their upkeep.
10.11: In whatever town or village you enter, find a trustworthy person and stay with them until you move on.
10.12: When you enter a house, give it your blessing.
10.13: If the household welcomes it, let your blessing remain. If they don't deserve it, take it back.
10.14: If any household or town refuses to welcome you or hear your message, shake the dust off your feet as you leave.
10.15: I tell you plainly, Sodom and Gomorrah will fare better on judgment day than that town.
10.16: Listen—I'm sending you out like sheep into a pack of wolves. So be as shrewd as snakes and as guileless as doves.
10.17: Be on your guard against people—they will hand you over to local courts and have you flogged in their synagogues.
10.18: You will stand before governors and kings as witnesses to them and to the Gentile world—and that will happen because of me.
10.19: When they arrest you, don't worry about what to say or how to say it. At that moment you'll be given the right words—
10.20: because it won't be you speaking, but your Father's Spirit speaking through you.
10.21: Brother will betray brother to death, fathers will betray their own children, and children will turn against parents and have them executed.
10.22: You will be hated by everyone because of me. But whoever endures to the end will be saved.
10.23: When they persecute you in one town, flee to the next. I tell you plainly, you won't finish going through all the towns of Israel before the Son of Man comes.
10.24: A student is not above his teacher, nor a servant above his master.
10.25: It's enough for students to become like their teacher and servants like their master. If they called the master of the house Beelzebul, how much worse will they treat the people of his household!
10.26: Don't be afraid of them. Everything that is hidden will be brought to light; everything concealed will become known.
10.27: What I tell you in private, speak publicly in broad daylight. What you hear whispered, shout from the rooftops.
10.28: Don't fear those who can only kill the body but cannot touch the soul. Fear the One who has power to destroy both soul and body in Gehenna.
10.29: Aren't two sparrows sold for a penny? Yet not one of them falls to the ground without your Father taking notice.
10.30: Even every hair on your head has been counted.
10.31: So don't be afraid—you are worth far more than a flock of sparrows.
10.32: Whoever openly identifies with me before others, I will openly identify before my Father in heaven.
10.33: But whoever disowns me before others, I will disown before my Father in heaven.
10.34: Don't think I've come to bring peaceful harmony to the world. I've come not to bring peace but a sword.
10.35: I've come to divide son from father, daughter from mother, daughter-in-law from mother-in-law—
10.36: a person's enemies will be the members of their own family.
10.37: Anyone who loves father or mother more than they love me is not worthy of me. Anyone who loves son or daughter more than me is not worthy of me.
10.38: Anyone who won't pick up their cross and come after me is not worthy of me.
10.39: The person who clings to their life will lose it. The person who gives up their life for my sake will find it.
10.40: Whoever receives you receives me, and whoever receives me receives the one who sent me.
10.41: Whoever welcomes a prophet because he is a prophet will receive a prophet's reward. Whoever welcomes a righteous person because they are righteous will receive a righteous person's reward.
10.42: And whoever gives even a cup of cold water to one of these little ones because they are my disciple—I tell you truly, they will not lose their reward.

11.1: When Jesus finished giving his twelve disciples their instructions, he moved on to teach and preach in the towns of the region.
11.2: John, in prison, heard about what the Messiah was doing and sent his disciples to ask,
11.3: Are you the one who is coming, or should we be waiting for someone else?
11.4: Jesus replied: Go back and tell John what you're hearing and seeing:
11.5: The blind see, the lame walk, those with skin disease are healed, the deaf hear, the dead are raised, and the poor are receiving the good news.
11.6: And how favored is the person who doesn't stumble over who I am.
11.7: As John's disciples left, Jesus began speaking to the crowd about John: When you went out to the wilderness, what were you looking for? A reed blown about by the wind?
11.8: Then what did you go out to see? A man dressed in fine clothes? People who wear fine clothes live in palaces.
11.9: So what did you go to see? A prophet? Yes—and I tell you, more than a prophet.
11.10: This is the one Scripture speaks of: I will send my messenger ahead of you, who will clear the path before you.
11.11: I tell you plainly: no one born of woman has been greater than John the Baptist. And yet the least person in the heavenly kingdom is greater than he is.
11.12: From the days of John the Baptist until now, the kingdom of heaven has been advancing with force, and forceful people have been pressing into it.
11.13: All the Prophets and the Law pointed forward until John.
11.14: If you're willing to accept it, he is the Elijah who was to come.
11.15: If you have ears, use them.
11.16: How shall I describe this generation? They're like children in the marketplace calling to their playmates:
11.17: We played happy music but you wouldn't dance; we played funeral music but you wouldn't mourn.
11.18: John came fasting, and they said he had a demon.
11.19: The Son of Man came eating and drinking, and they say: Look at him—a glutton and a drunkard, a friend of tax collectors and sinners! But wisdom is vindicated by what it produces.
11.20: Then Jesus began to condemn the towns where most of his miracles had been done, because they refused to repent.
11.21: How terrible it will be for you, Chorazin! How terrible for you, Bethsaida! If the miracles done among you had been done in Tyre and Sidon, those cities would have turned to God long ago in sackcloth and ashes.
11.22: But I tell you, Tyre and Sidon will fare better on judgment day than you will.
11.23: And you, Capernaum—do you think you'll be raised to heaven? You'll be brought down to the realm of the dead. If the miracles performed in you had been done in Sodom, it would still be standing today.
11.24: But I tell you—Sodom will fare better on judgment day than you.
11.25: At that time Jesus said: I praise you, Father, Lord of heaven and earth, because you have hidden these things from the wise and intelligent and revealed them to those who are childlike.
11.26: Yes, Father—this is what pleased you.
11.27: My Father has entrusted everything to me. No one truly knows the Son except the Father, and no one truly knows the Father except the Son—and those to whom the Son chooses to reveal him.
11.28: Come to me, all of you who are worn out and carrying heavy loads, and I will give you rest.
11.29: Take on my yoke and learn from me, for I am gentle and humble at heart, and you will find rest for your souls.
11.30: My yoke is comfortable and my burden is light.

12.1: Around that time Jesus was walking through grainfields on the Sabbath. His disciples were hungry and started picking heads of grain to eat.
12.2: The Pharisees saw this and objected: Your disciples are doing what is against the law on the Sabbath.
12.3: Jesus replied: Haven't you read what David did when he and his men were hungry?
12.4: He went into the house of God and ate the sacred bread—which neither he nor his men were permitted to eat, only the priests.
12.5: Or haven't you read in the Law that the temple priests break the Sabbath every week—and are completely innocent?
12.6: I tell you, something greater than the temple is standing right here.
12.7: If you had understood what this means—I want compassion, not religious ritual—you wouldn't have condemned people who are innocent.
12.8: For the Son of Man is master of the Sabbath.
12.9: Moving on from there, he entered their synagogue.
12.10: A man was there with a withered hand. Looking for grounds to accuse him, they asked: Is it permitted to heal on the Sabbath?
12.11: He said: If any one of you has a sheep that falls into a ditch on the Sabbath, won't you grab it and pull it out?
12.12: How much more valuable is a human being than a sheep! Of course it's right to do good on the Sabbath.
12.13: Then he said to the man: Stretch out your hand. He did, and it was completely restored, as healthy as the other.
12.14: The Pharisees walked out and immediately began plotting how to eliminate Jesus.
12.15: Aware of their plot, Jesus slipped away. Large crowds followed him, and he healed all of them.
12.16: He warned them not to publicize who he was.
12.18: Here is my servant, chosen by me, the one I love and in whom I take delight. I will put my Spirit on him and he will announce justice to the nations.
12.19: He will not quarrel or shout; no one will hear him raising his voice in the streets.
12.20: A bent reed he will not snap off; a flickering wick he will not snuff out—until he has led justice to triumph.
12.21: And the nations will place their hope in his name.
12.22: Then they brought Jesus a man who was demon-possessed—blind and unable to speak. Jesus healed him so that he could both speak and see.
12.23: All the crowds were astonished: Could this be the Son of David?
12.24: But when the Pharisees heard it, they said: He only drives out demons because he's in league with Beelzebul, the ruler of demons.
12.25: Jesus knew what they were thinking: Any kingdom torn apart by internal conflict collapses. Any city or household split against itself cannot survive.
12.26: If Satan is driving out Satan, he's fighting against himself—how would his kingdom survive?
12.27: If I drive out demons by Beelzebul, what power do your own followers use to drive them out? Their practice will be your judge.
12.28: But if I drive out demons by the Spirit of God, then God's kingdom has already arrived among you.
12.29: How can anyone break into a strong man's house and make off with his belongings unless he first ties up the strong man? Only then can the house be plundered.
12.30: Whoever is not on my side is against me, and whoever doesn't gather with me scatters.
12.31: So I tell you: every sin and slander can be forgiven, but speaking against the Holy Spirit will not be forgiven.
12.32: Anyone who speaks a word against the Son of Man can be forgiven. But anyone who speaks against the Holy Spirit will not be forgiven—not in this age, and not in the age to come.
12.33: A tree is either healthy—producing good fruit, or diseased—producing bad fruit. The tree is known by its fruit.
12.34: You snake-spawn! How could evil people like you say anything good? Whatever fills the heart overflows through the mouth.
12.35: A good person draws good things from the good stored within. An evil person draws evil from the evil within.
12.36: I tell you: people will give account on judgment day for every careless word they have spoken.
12.37: Your words will either vindicate you or condemn you.
12.38: Then some of the Pharisees and religious scholars said: Teacher, we want to see a miraculous sign from you.
12.39: He replied: A corrupt and unfaithful generation demands a sign—but the only sign it will be given is the sign of the prophet Jonah.
12.40: Just as Jonah was inside the great fish for three days and three nights, so the Son of Man will be in the heart of the earth for three days and three nights.
12.41: The people of Nineveh will stand up at the judgment and condemn this generation—because they turned to God when Jonah preached. And something far greater than Jonah is standing here.
12.42: The Queen of Sheba will stand up at the judgment and condemn this generation—she traveled from the ends of the earth to hear Solomon's wisdom. And something far greater than Solomon is standing here.
12.43: When an evil spirit leaves a person, it wanders through waterless wastelands looking for a place to rest and finds none.
12.44: Then it says: I'll go back to my house where I came from. When it gets there, it finds the house empty—swept clean and in perfect order.
12.45: Then it goes and brings back seven other spirits more evil than itself, and they all move in and settle down. That person ends up in a worse state than before. That's what will happen to this corrupt generation.
12.46: While he was still speaking to the crowds, his mother and brothers were standing outside, wanting to talk to him.
12.47: Someone told him: Your mother and brothers are outside asking for you.
12.48: Jesus replied: Who is my mother? Who are my brothers?
12.49: Gesturing toward his disciples, he said: Here are my mother and my brothers.
12.50: Whoever does what my Father in heaven requires—that person is my brother, my sister, my mother.

13.1: That same day Jesus left the house and went to sit by the lake.
13.2: Such enormous crowds gathered that he climbed into a boat and sat there, while the crowd stood on the shore.
13.3: He taught them many things through parables. He said: A farmer went out to plant his seed.
13.4: As he scattered the seed, some fell on the path, and the birds came and ate it up.
13.5: Some seed fell on rocky ground where the soil was thin. It sprouted quickly because the soil was shallow.
13.6: But when the sun rose, the young plants were scorched. Without deep roots they dried up and died.
13.7: Some seed fell in among thorn-bushes, which grew up and choked out the plants.
13.8: But some seed fell on good soil and produced a crop—a hundred times, sixty times, or thirty times what was planted.
13.9: If you have ears, use them.
13.10: His disciples came and asked: Why do you speak to the crowds in parables?
13.11: He replied: Because you have been given insight into the secrets of the heavenly kingdom, but they have not.
13.12: Those who already have will be given more—an overflow. But those who have nothing will lose even what little they think they have.
13.13: This is why I speak to them in parables: they look but don't really see; they listen but don't really hear or understand.
13.14: In them the prophecy of Isaiah is coming true: You will hear and hear—but never understand. You will look and look—but never see.
13.15: This people's heart has grown dull; their ears barely hear; they've shut their eyes tight—otherwise they would see with their eyes, hear with their ears, understand with their hearts, and turn to me and I would heal them.
13.16: But how favored you are—your eyes see and your ears hear.
13.17: I tell you plainly—many prophets and righteous people longed to see what you're seeing and didn't, and to hear what you're hearing and didn't.
13.18: Listen, then, to what the parable of the sower means.
13.19: When someone hears the message about the kingdom and doesn't grasp it, the evil one comes and steals away what was planted in their heart. This is the seed that fell on the path.
13.20: The seed on rocky ground describes someone who hears the message and immediately receives it with enthusiasm.
13.21: But it doesn't take root in them. They last only a short time—when hardship or persecution comes because of the message, they quickly collapse.
13.22: The seed in the thorns describes someone who hears the message, but life's anxieties and the seductive promises of wealth crowd it out, and it produces nothing.
13.23: But the seed on good soil describes someone who hears the message and understands it—who actually produces a crop, yielding thirty, sixty, or a hundred times what was sown.
13.24: He presented them with another parable: The kingdom of heaven is like a farmer who planted good seed in his field.
13.25: But while everyone was asleep, his enemy came and scattered weeds among the wheat, then slipped away.
13.26: When the wheat sprouted and began to form grain, the weeds appeared too.
13.27: The farmhand came to the owner: Sir, didn't you plant good seed? Where did all these weeds come from?
13.28: An enemy did this, he said. The workers asked: Do you want us to go pull them out?
13.29: No, he said, because when you pull the weeds you might uproot the wheat along with them.
13.30: Let them both grow together until harvest time. Then I'll tell the harvesters: First pull out the weeds and bundle them up for burning. Then bring the wheat into my barn.
13.31: He gave them another parable: The kingdom of heaven is like a mustard seed that a man planted in his field.
13.32: It's the smallest of all seeds—but when it grows, it becomes the largest of garden plants, almost a tree, so that birds come and nest in its branches.
13.33: He told them another parable: The kingdom of heaven is like yeast that a woman took and worked into a large amount of flour—until the whole batch was leavened.
13.34: Jesus spoke all of this to the crowds in parables—he said nothing to them without using a parable.
13.35: This fulfilled the words of the prophet: I will speak in parables; I will announce things kept secret since the creation of the world.
13.36: Then he left the crowds and went inside. His disciples came to him: Explain the parable of the weeds.
13.37: He replied: The one who planted the good seed is the Son of Man.
13.38: The field is the world. The good seed are those who belong to the kingdom. The weeds are those who belong to the evil one.
13.39: The enemy who planted them is the devil. The harvest is the end of the age, and the harvesters are angels.
13.40: Just as the weeds are pulled up and burned, so it will be at the end of the age.
13.41: The Son of Man will send out his angels and they will remove from his kingdom everything that causes people to stumble, and all who practice lawlessness.
13.42: They will throw them into the blazing furnace—where there will be weeping and grinding of teeth.
13.43: Then the righteous will shine like the sun in their Father's kingdom. If you have ears, use them.
13.44: The kingdom of heaven is like treasure buried in a field. A man discovers it, buries it again, and in his excitement goes and sells everything he has to buy that field.
13.45: Again, the kingdom of heaven is like a merchant searching for fine pearls.
13.46: When he finds one pearl of extraordinary value, he goes and sells everything he owns to buy it.
13.47: Again, the kingdom of heaven is like a large fishing net thrown into the sea, pulling in fish of every kind.
13.48: When it's full, the fishermen haul it to shore, sit down, and sort it out—keeping the good fish and throwing out the bad.
13.49: That's how it will be at the end of the age. The angels will come and separate the evil from the righteous,
13.50: and throw them into the blazing furnace—where there will be weeping and grinding of teeth.
13.51: Have you understood all this? he asked. Yes, they said.
13.52: He replied: So every scholar who has become a student of the heavenly kingdom is like a homeowner who brings out of his storehouse things both new and old.
13.53: When Jesus finished these parables, he left that place.
13.54: He returned to his hometown and taught in their synagogue. The people were astonished: Where does he get this wisdom and these miraculous powers?
13.55: Isn't this the carpenter's son? Isn't his mother named Mary? Don't his brothers include James, Joseph, Simon, and Judas?
13.56: And all his sisters live right here among us. Where does he get all this?
13.57: They were offended by him. But Jesus said: A prophet is never without honor—except in his hometown and among his own family.
13.58: He did very few miracles there because of their unbelief.

14.1: Around that time, Herod the ruler heard reports about Jesus.
14.2: He said to his servants: This has to be John the Baptist—he's risen from the dead! That's why these miraculous powers are working through him.
14.3: Herod had arrested John, chained him, and put him in prison because of Herodias, the wife of his brother Philip,
14.4: because John kept telling him: It's against God's law for you to have her.
14.5: Herod wanted to execute John but was afraid of the people—they regarded John as a prophet.
14.6: When Herod's birthday celebration came, the daughter of Herodias danced for the guests and delighted Herod so much
14.7: that he promised on oath to give her anything she wanted.
14.8: Coached by her mother, she said: Give me right now the head of John the Baptist on a platter.
14.9: The king was deeply troubled, but because of his oath and his guests, he ordered that her request be granted.
14.10: He sent word to the prison and had John beheaded.
14.11: John's head was brought in on a platter and given to the girl, who took it to her mother.
14.12: John's disciples came, took away his body and buried it, then went and told Jesus.
14.13: When Jesus heard the news, he withdrew by boat to a quiet, secluded place. But when the crowds heard, they followed on foot from the towns.
14.14: When Jesus stepped ashore and saw the vast crowd, his heart broke for them, and he healed those among them who were sick.
14.15: As evening came, his disciples approached: This is a remote area and it's getting late. Send the crowds away so they can go to the villages and buy themselves food.
14.16: Jesus replied: They don't need to go anywhere. You give them something to eat.
14.17: All we have here, they said, is five loaves of bread and two fish.
14.18: Bring them here to me, he said.
14.19: He told the crowds to sit down on the grass. Taking the five loaves and two fish, he looked up to heaven, gave thanks, broke the loaves and handed them to the disciples—who distributed them to the crowds.
14.20: Everyone ate until they were full. Afterward the disciples collected twelve basketfuls of leftovers.
14.21: The number of men who ate was about five thousand, not counting women and children.
14.22: Jesus immediately directed his disciples to get in the boat and cross to the other side while he dismissed the crowds.
14.23: After sending them away, he went up the hillside alone to pray. By evening he was there by himself.
14.24: Meanwhile the boat was far out on the lake, battered by waves—the wind was against them.
14.25: Just before dawn Jesus came to them, walking on the water.
14.26: When the disciples saw him walking on the water, they were terrified. It's a ghost! they shouted in fear.
14.27: But Jesus spoke to them at once: Take courage—it's me. Don't be afraid.
14.28: Peter called out: Lord, if it's really you, tell me to come to you on the water.
14.29: Come, Jesus said. Peter climbed out of the boat and began walking on the water toward Jesus.
14.30: But when he noticed the fierce wind, fear gripped him and he started sinking. Lord, save me! he cried.
14.31: Jesus immediately reached out and caught him: You of such little faith—why did you hesitate?
14.32: When they climbed into the boat, the wind stopped.
14.33: Those in the boat worshipped him: Truly you are the Son of God.
14.34: When they crossed the lake, they landed at Gennesaret.
14.35: When the people there recognized Jesus, they sent word throughout the surrounding region, and people brought all the sick to him.
14.36: They begged him to let the sick just touch the fringe of his garment—and all who touched it were healed.

15.1: Some Pharisees and religious scholars came from Jerusalem to challenge Jesus.
15.2: Why do your disciples violate the tradition of the elders? They don't wash their hands before eating!
15.3: Jesus shot back: And why do you break God's commandment for the sake of your tradition?
15.4: God said: Honor your father and mother—and whoever speaks evil against father or mother must be put to death.
15.5: But you say that if someone declares their resources dedicated to God,
15.6: they're released from the obligation to care for their parents. You have completely cancelled God's word with your tradition.
15.7: You hypocrites—Isaiah described you exactly when he wrote:
15.8: These people honor me with words, but their hearts are nowhere near me.
15.9: Their worship is hollow—they teach man-made rules as if they were God's commands.
15.10: Jesus called the crowd back and said: Listen and get this straight.
15.11: It's not what goes into your mouth that makes you unclean before God—it's what comes out of your mouth.
15.12: His disciples came to him: Do you know the Pharisees were insulted by what you said?
15.13: He replied: Every plant that my heavenly Father didn't plant will be uprooted.
15.14: Leave them alone. They are blind guides—and when one blind person leads another, they both fall into a ditch.
15.15: Peter said: Explain this to us.
15.16: Jesus replied: Are you still not getting it?
15.17: Don't you understand that whatever goes into the mouth travels to the stomach and then leaves the body?
15.18: But what comes out of the mouth originates in the heart—and that's what makes a person unclean.
15.19: Because out of the heart come evil thoughts, murder, adultery, sexual immorality, theft, false testimony, and slander.
15.20: These are the things that make a person unclean. But eating with unwashed hands doesn't.
15.21: Jesus left that area and withdrew to the region of Tyre and Sidon.
15.22: A Canaanite woman from that area came and cried out: Lord, Son of David, have mercy on me! My daughter is tormented by a demon.
15.23: Jesus said nothing. His disciples came and urged him: Send her away—she won't stop shouting after us.
15.24: Jesus replied: My mission is specifically to the lost sheep of Israel.
15.25: But she came and knelt at his feet: Lord, help me!
15.26: He replied: It isn't right to take the children's bread and throw it to the dogs.
15.27: Yes, Lord, she said—but even dogs eat the scraps that fall from their master's table.
15.28: Jesus replied: Woman, your faith is remarkable. What you asked will be done. And her daughter was healed from that moment.
15.29: Jesus moved on from there and went along the shore of the Sea of Galilee. He climbed a hillside and sat down.
15.30: Huge crowds came to him, bringing the lame, blind, crippled, and mute, and many others. They laid them at his feet, and he healed them.
15.31: The crowds were astonished—the mute were speaking, the crippled were healed, the lame were walking, and the blind were seeing. They praised the God of Israel.
15.32: Jesus called his disciples: I feel deeply for these people—they've been with me three days and have nothing to eat. I don't want to send them away hungry or they'll collapse before they get home.
15.33: His disciples replied: Where would we find enough bread in this remote place to feed this crowd?
15.34: How many loaves do you have? Jesus asked. Seven, they said, plus a few small fish.
15.35: He told the crowd to sit on the ground.
15.36: He took the seven loaves and the fish, gave thanks, broke them, and gave them to his disciples—who distributed them to the crowd.
15.37: Everyone ate until full. The disciples collected seven large basketfuls of leftovers.
15.38: The men alone numbered four thousand, not counting women and children.
15.39: After dismissing the crowds, Jesus got in the boat and went to the region of Magadan.

16.1: The Pharisees and Sadducees came to test Jesus, demanding that he show them a sign from heaven.
16.2: He replied: In the evening you say, Fine weather coming—the sky is red.
16.3: In the morning: Stormy today—the sky is red and threatening. You can read the signs in the sky, but you can't read the signs of what God is doing right now.
16.4: A corrupt, unfaithful generation demands signs—but the only sign it will get is the sign of Jonah. And he walked away and left them.
16.5: When they crossed to the other side, the disciples realized they had forgotten to bring bread.
16.6: Jesus warned them: Watch out—be on guard against the yeast of the Pharisees and Sadducees.
16.7: They began discussing this among themselves: He must mean we didn't bring bread.
16.8: Jesus knew what they were thinking: You of such little faith—why are you focused on the fact that you have no bread?
16.9: Don't you grasp it yet? Don't you remember the five loaves for five thousand—and how many baskets you filled?
16.10: Or the seven loaves for four thousand—and how many large baskets you collected?
16.11: How do you still not see that I wasn't talking about bread? Be on guard against the yeast of the Pharisees and Sadducees.
16.12: Then they understood he wasn't talking about bread yeast but about the teaching of the Pharisees and Sadducees.
16.13: When Jesus arrived in the region of Caesarea Philippi, he asked his disciples: Who do people say the Son of Man is?
16.14: They replied: Some say John the Baptist. Others say Elijah. Still others say Jeremiah or one of the prophets.
16.15: He pressed them: But who do you say I am?
16.16: Simon Peter said: You are the Messiah—the Son of the living God.
16.17: Jesus replied: How blessed you are, Simon son of Jonah—this wasn't revealed to you by human insight but by my Father in heaven.
16.18: And I tell you this: you are Peter—the Rock—and on this rock I will build my community, and not even death's gates will be able to stand against it.
16.19: I will give you the keys to the heavenly kingdom. Whatever you bind on earth will be bound in heaven, and whatever you release on earth will be released in heaven.
16.20: Then he instructed his disciples to tell no one that he was the Messiah.
16.21: From that point Jesus began to tell his disciples plainly: He must go to Jerusalem, suffer greatly at the hands of the elders, chief priests, and religious scholars, be killed—and on the third day be raised.
16.22: Peter pulled him aside and began to rebuke him: God forbid, Lord! This must never happen to you!
16.23: Jesus turned on him: Get behind me, Satan! You are a stumbling block to me—you're thinking like a human, not like God.
16.24: Then Jesus said to his disciples: Anyone who wants to follow me must say no to their own desires, shoulder their cross, and come after me.
16.25: Whoever tries to hold onto their life will lose it—but whoever gives their life away for my sake will find it.
16.26: What does a person gain if they win the whole world but lose their own soul? And what could they ever offer to buy it back?
16.27: Because the Son of Man will come in his Father's glory with his angels—and then he will repay each person according to what they've done.
16.28: I tell you truly—some of those standing here will not die before they see the Son of Man arriving in his kingly power.

17.1: Six days later Jesus took Peter, James, and John up a high mountain by themselves.
17.2: There, right before their eyes, his appearance was transformed. His face blazed like the sun, and his clothing became dazzling white as light.
17.3: Suddenly Moses and Elijah appeared and were speaking with him.
17.4: Peter said to Jesus: Lord, it's wonderful that we're here! If you want, I'll set up three shelters—one for you, one for Moses, and one for Elijah.
17.5: While he was still speaking, a radiant cloud enveloped them, and a voice from the cloud said: This is my Son, the one I love and in whom I delight. Listen to him!
17.6: When the disciples heard this, they threw themselves face-down on the ground in terror.
17.7: Jesus came and touched them: Get up. Don't be afraid.
17.8: When they raised their eyes, they saw only Jesus.
17.9: As they descended the mountain, Jesus instructed them: Don't tell anyone what you've seen until the Son of Man is raised from the dead.
17.10: The disciples asked: Why do the religious scholars say that Elijah must come first?
17.11: Jesus replied: Elijah does come first and will restore everything.
17.12: But I tell you—Elijah has already come, and they didn't recognize him. They did whatever they wanted to him. And the Son of Man will suffer at their hands in the same way.
17.13: Then the disciples realized he was talking about John the Baptist.
17.14: When they rejoined the crowd, a man came and knelt before Jesus.
17.15: Lord, have mercy on my son. He has seizures and suffers terribly—he often falls into fire or water.
17.16: I brought him to your disciples but they couldn't heal him.
17.17: Jesus said: You faithless, twisted generation—how long must I stay with you? How long must I put up with you? Bring him here.
17.18: Jesus rebuked the demon and it left the boy, who was healed from that moment.
17.19: The disciples came to Jesus privately: Why couldn't we drive it out?
17.20: He replied: Because of your small faith. I tell you truly—if your faith were even the size of a mustard seed, you could say to this mountain: Move from here to there—and it would move. Nothing would be impossible.
17.21: But this kind only comes out through prayer and fasting.
17.22: While they were gathered in Galilee, Jesus said: The Son of Man is about to be handed over into human hands.
17.23: They will kill him—and on the third day he will be raised. The disciples were filled with grief.
17.24: When they arrived in Capernaum, the temple tax collectors came to Peter: Does your teacher pay the temple tax?
17.25: Yes, he does, Peter said. When he got home, Jesus spoke first: What do you think, Simon? From whom do earthly kings collect taxes—from their own sons or from others?
17.26: From others, Peter said. Then the sons are free, Jesus replied.
17.27: But so we don't give offence—go to the lake, throw in a line, and take the first fish you catch. Open its mouth and you'll find a coin worth exactly enough for both our taxes. Take it and pay them.

18.1: At that point the disciples came to Jesus with a question: Who is the greatest in the heavenly kingdom?
18.2: He called a small child over and stood the child in the middle of them.
18.3: I tell you plainly: unless you completely turn around and become like children, you will never enter the heavenly kingdom.
18.4: So whoever makes themselves as humble as this child is the greatest in the heavenly kingdom.
18.5: And whoever welcomes a child like this in my name, welcomes me.
18.6: But if anyone causes one of these little ones who trust in me to stumble, they would be better off with a heavy millstone hung around their neck and thrown into the deep sea.
18.7: How terrible it will be for the world because of what causes people to stumble! Such stumbling blocks must come—but how terrible for the person who causes them!
18.8: If your hand or foot is causing you to sin, cut it off and throw it away. Better to enter life crippled than to have both hands and feet and be thrown into eternal fire.
18.9: And if your eye is causing you to sin, gouge it out and throw it away. Better to enter life with one eye than to have two eyes and be thrown into Gehenna's fire.
18.10: Make sure you don't look down on one of these little ones. I tell you, their angels in heaven are continually in the presence of my Father.
18.11: The Son of Man came to save the lost.
18.12: What do you think? If a man has a hundred sheep and one wanders away, won't he leave the ninety-nine on the hillside and go search for the lost one?
18.13: And when he finds it, I tell you—his joy over that one sheep is greater than his joy over the ninety-nine who never wandered.
18.14: In the same way, your Father in heaven does not want even one of these little ones to be lost.
18.15: If a fellow believer sins against you, go to them privately and point it out. If they listen, you've restored the relationship.
18.16: If they won't listen, bring one or two others—so that every word can be confirmed by two or three witnesses.
18.17: If they refuse to listen even to others, bring it before the community. And if they refuse even the community, treat them as you would an outsider.
18.18: I tell you truly—whatever you bind on earth will be bound in heaven, and whatever you release on earth will be released in heaven.
18.19: Furthermore, if two of you agree together on earth about anything you ask for, my Father in heaven will do it for you.
18.20: Because where two or three gather in my name, I am right there among them.
18.21: Peter came to Jesus with a question: Lord, how many times should I forgive someone who wrongs me? Seven times?
18.22: Jesus answered: Not seven times—seventy times seven.
18.23: So the heavenly kingdom is like a king who decided to settle his accounts with his servants.
18.24: When he began the process, a man was brought before him who owed ten thousand talents—an impossibly large sum.
18.25: Since he couldn't pay, the master ordered him to be sold—along with his wife, children, and everything he owned—to settle the debt.
18.26: The servant fell to his knees before him: Be patient with me, he begged, and I'll pay everything back.
18.27: The master felt compassion for him, cancelled the entire debt, and released him.
18.28: But that same servant went out and found a colleague who owed him a hundred denarii—a small amount. He grabbed him by the throat: Pay back what you owe me!
18.29: His colleague fell to his knees and begged: Be patient with me and I'll pay you back.
18.30: But he refused. He had the man thrown in prison until the debt was paid.
18.31: When the other servants witnessed this, they were deeply disturbed. They went and reported everything to the master.
18.32: The master summoned him: You wicked servant! I cancelled your entire debt when you begged me.
18.33: Shouldn't you have shown your colleague the same mercy I showed you?
18.34: In his fury, the master handed him over to the jailers to be tortured until he had paid back everything.
18.35: This is what my heavenly Father will do to you if you don't forgive your brother or sister from your heart.

19.1: When Jesus had finished speaking, he left Galilee and traveled to the region of Judea on the far side of the Jordan.
19.2: Large crowds followed him, and he healed them there.
19.3: Pharisees came to test him: Is it legal for a man to divorce his wife for any reason?
19.4: He replied: Haven't you read that from the beginning the Creator made them male and female,
19.5: and said: For this reason a man will leave his father and mother and be joined to his wife, and the two shall become one flesh?
19.6: So they are no longer two people but one. What God has joined together, no human being should separate.
19.7: Then why, they asked, did Moses command that a man give his wife a written divorce notice?
19.8: Jesus replied: Moses allowed you to divorce your wives because of your moral stubbornness—but that was never God's original intention.
19.9: I tell you: anyone who divorces his wife—except for sexual unfaithfulness—and marries another, commits adultery.
19.10: The disciples said: If that's the situation between a man and his wife, it's better not to get married at all.
19.11: Jesus replied: Not everyone can accept this—only those to whom it has been given.
19.12: Some people are not wired for marriage from birth; some have been made that way by others; and some choose to forgo marriage for the sake of God's kingdom. Whoever is able to accept this should accept it.
19.13: People were bringing small children to Jesus so he could place his hands on them and pray. The disciples turned them away.
19.14: Jesus said: Let the children come to me—don't stop them. The kingdom of heaven belongs to people like these.
19.15: He placed his hands on them and then moved on.
19.16: Then a man came to him: Teacher, what good thing must I do to receive eternal life?
19.17: Jesus replied: Why do you ask me about what is good? There is only One who is good. If you want to enter into life, keep the commandments.
19.18: Which ones? the man asked. Jesus said: Don't murder, don't commit adultery, don't steal, don't lie in court,
19.19: honor your father and mother, and love your neighbor as yourself.
19.20: I've kept all these, the young man said. What am I still missing?
19.21: Jesus replied: If you want to be complete, go sell everything you own and give the money to the poor—you'll have treasure in heaven. Then come and follow me.
19.22: When the young man heard this, he walked away sad—because he was very wealthy.
19.23: Jesus said to his disciples: I tell you truly—it is very hard for a rich person to enter God's kingdom.
19.24: In fact, it's easier for a camel to go through the eye of a needle than for a wealthy person to enter God's kingdom.
19.25: The disciples were stunned: Then who on earth can be saved?
19.26: Jesus looked at them steadily: With human beings it's impossible—but with God, everything is possible.
19.27: Peter spoke up: We've left everything to follow you. What will we receive?
19.28: Jesus replied: I tell you truly—when everything is made new and the Son of Man sits on his glorious throne, you who have followed me will also sit on twelve thrones, ruling over the twelve tribes of Israel.
19.29: Everyone who has left houses, brothers, sisters, father, mother, children, or land for my sake will receive a hundred times as much—and will inherit the life of the coming age.
19.30: But many who are first will be last, and many who are last will be first.

20.1: The kingdom of heaven is like a landowner who went out at dawn to hire workers for his vineyard.
20.2: He agreed to pay them a denarius for the day and sent them to work.
20.3: Around nine in the morning he went out and saw others standing idle in the marketplace.
20.4: He told them: Go to the vineyard, and I'll pay you what's fair. They went.
20.5: He did the same at noon and at three in the afternoon.
20.6: Around five in the afternoon he went out and found still more standing around. He asked: Why have you been idle all day?
20.7: Nobody hired us, they replied. He said: Go to the vineyard then.
20.8: When evening came, the vineyard owner told his foreman: Call the workers and pay them, starting with those hired last and going to those hired first.
20.9: Those hired at five o'clock came and each received a denarius.
20.10: When those hired first came, they expected more—but each of them also received one denarius.
20.11: They took it and grumbled at the landowner.
20.12: These people who were hired last worked only one hour, and you've paid them the same as us—we who endured the whole day in the blazing heat!
20.13: The landowner replied to one of them: Friend, I'm not cheating you. Didn't you agree to work for a denarius?
20.14: Take what belongs to you and go. I choose to give the last person hired the same as you.
20.15: Don't I have the right to do what I want with what's mine? Or is your eye filled with envy because I am generous?
20.16: So the last will be first, and the first will be last.
20.17: As Jesus was going up to Jerusalem, he took the twelve disciples aside privately and said to them:
20.18: We are going up to Jerusalem. The Son of Man will be handed over to the chief priests and religious scholars. They will sentence him to death
20.19: and turn him over to the Gentiles to be mocked, flogged, and crucified. On the third day he will be raised.
20.20: Then the mother of Zebedee's sons came to Jesus with her sons and bowed before him with a request.
20.21: He asked: What do you want? She replied: Promise me that in your kingdom these two sons of mine will sit at your right and left hand.
20.22: Jesus replied: You don't know what you're asking. Can you drink the cup I am about to drink? We can, they said.
20.23: Jesus said: You will drink my cup—but the seats at my right and left are not mine to give. They belong to those for whom my Father has prepared them.
20.24: When the other ten heard this, they were furious with the two brothers.
20.25: Jesus called them together: You know that the rulers of the Gentiles dominate their people—and their leaders use authority as a tool of power.
20.26: That's not how things work among you. Whoever wants to be great among you must be your servant.
20.27: And whoever wants to be first must be everyone's slave.
20.28: Just as the Son of Man didn't come to be served but to serve—and to give his life as the ransom price for many.
20.29: As Jesus and his disciples were leaving Jericho, a large crowd followed them.
20.30: Two blind men were sitting by the roadside. When they heard Jesus was passing, they shouted: Lord, Son of David, have mercy on us!
20.31: The crowd tried to silence them, but they only shouted louder: Lord, Son of David, have mercy on us!
20.32: Jesus stopped and called them: What do you want me to do for you?
20.33: Lord, they answered, we want to see.
20.34: Jesus felt deep compassion and touched their eyes. Instantly they could see, and they followed him.

21.1: As they neared Jerusalem and reached Bethphage on the Mount of Olives, Jesus sent two disciples ahead.
21.2: Go to the village just ahead, he said. You'll find a donkey tied up and her colt beside her. Untie them and bring them to me.
21.3: If anyone questions you, just say: The Lord needs them—and they'll let you take them.
21.4: This happened to fulfill the words of the prophet:
21.5: Tell the daughter of Zion: Your King is coming to you—humble, riding on a donkey, the foal of a working animal.
21.6: The disciples went and did as Jesus had told them.
21.7: They brought the donkey and colt, spread their cloaks on them, and Jesus sat on them.
21.8: A very large crowd spread their cloaks on the road; others cut branches from trees and scattered them in the path.
21.9: The crowds going ahead and those following behind all shouted: Hosanna to the Son of David! Blessed is the one who comes in the name of the Lord! Hosanna in the highest!
21.10: When Jesus entered Jerusalem, the whole city was in an uproar: Who is this?
21.11: The crowds said: This is Jesus, the prophet from Nazareth in Galilee.
21.12: Jesus entered the temple and drove out everyone who was buying and selling there. He overturned the money-changers' tables and the seats of those selling doves.
21.13: He said: Scripture says, My house will be called a house of prayer—but you've turned it into a robbers' hideout.
21.14: The blind and lame came to him in the temple and he healed them.
21.15: When the chief priests and religious scholars saw the miracles and heard children in the temple shouting Hosanna to the Son of David, they were indignant.
21.16: They demanded: Do you hear what these children are saying? Yes, Jesus replied. Haven't you ever read: From the mouths of children and infants you have prepared praise?
21.17: And he left them, went out to Bethany, and spent the night there.
21.18: Early the next morning as he was returning to the city, he was hungry.
21.19: He saw a fig tree by the road and went to it, but found nothing but leaves. He said to it: May you never produce fruit again! And the fig tree immediately withered.
21.20: The disciples were astonished: How did the fig tree wither so quickly?
