21.21: Jesus replied: I tell you truly—if you have faith and don't doubt, you can do not only what was done to this fig tree, but even say to this mountain: Be lifted up and hurled into the sea—and it will happen.
21.22: Everything you ask for in prayer—if you believe—you will receive.
21.23: When he had entered the temple and was teaching, the chief priests and elders came to him: By what authority are you doing these things? Who gave you this authority?
21.24: Jesus replied: I'll ask you one question too—if you answer me, I'll tell you by what authority I do these things.
21.25: John's baptism—was it from heaven or from human beings? They debated privately: If we say from heaven, he'll ask why we didn't believe him.
21.26: But if we say from human beings, we're afraid of the crowd—everyone considers John a prophet.
21.27: So they answered: We don't know. Then Jesus said: Neither will I tell you by what authority I do these things.
21.28: What do you think? A man had two sons. He went to the first and said: Son, go work in the vineyard today.
21.29: I won't, the son replied—but later he changed his mind and went.
21.30: The father went to the other son and said the same thing. He answered: Sure, I'll go—but he never did.
21.31: Which of the two did what his father wanted? The first one, they said. I tell you plainly, Jesus replied: tax collectors and prostitutes are entering God's kingdom ahead of you.
21.32: John came to show you the path of righteousness, and you refused to believe him. But the tax collectors and prostitutes did believe him. And even after seeing that, you didn't change your minds and believe.
21.33: Listen to another parable. A landowner planted a vineyard, built a wall around it, dug a winepress, and erected a watchtower. Then he leased it to tenant farmers and went abroad.
21.34: When harvest time came, he sent his servants to collect his share of the produce.
21.35: The tenants seized his servants—they beat one, killed another, and stoned a third.
21.36: He sent more servants—more than before—and they were treated the same way.
21.37: Finally he sent his son: They will respect my son, he thought.
21.38: But when the tenants saw the son coming, they said to each other: This is the heir—let's kill him and take his inheritance for ourselves!
21.39: They seized him, threw him out of the vineyard, and murdered him.
21.40: When the vineyard owner comes, what will he do to those tenants?
21.41: He will bring those wretches to a wretched end, they said, and lease the vineyard to other farmers who will give him his rightful share at harvest.
21.42: Jesus said: Haven't you read in the Scriptures: The stone the builders threw away has become the cornerstone. The Lord did this, and it is astonishing to us.
21.43: I tell you: God's kingdom will be taken from you and given to a people who will produce its fruit.
21.44: Anyone who stumbles on this stone will be shattered. And anyone it falls on will be crushed to dust.
21.45: When the chief priests and Pharisees heard his parables, they realized he was talking about them.
21.46: They were looking for a way to arrest him—but they feared the crowds, who regarded him as a prophet.

22.1: Jesus continued teaching them in parables.
22.2: The kingdom of heaven is like a king who threw a wedding banquet for his son.
22.3: He sent his servants to summon the invited guests—but they refused to come.
22.4: He sent more servants with this message: Tell the guests everything is ready—the banquet is prepared, come now.
22.5: But they ignored it and went about their business—one to his farm, another to his shop.
22.6: The rest seized his servants, abused them, and killed them.
22.7: The king was furious. He sent his army, destroyed those murderers, and burned their city.
22.8: Then he said to his servants: The banquet is ready, but the invited guests weren't worthy.
22.9: Go to the street corners and invite anyone you find.
22.10: The servants went out and brought in everyone they could find—good and bad alike—and the banquet hall was packed.
22.11: When the king came in to greet the guests, he spotted a man not wearing wedding clothes.
22.12: Friend, he said, how did you get in here without proper wedding attire? The man had nothing to say.
22.13: The king told his attendants: Bind him hand and foot and throw him into the outer darkness—where there is weeping and grinding of teeth.
22.14: Many are invited—but few are chosen.
22.15: The Pharisees left and plotted how to trap him with his own words.
22.16: They sent their disciples along with some Herodians to say: Teacher, we know you are truthful and teach God's way honestly—you don't play favorites or care what anyone thinks.
22.17: So tell us—is it right to pay taxes to Caesar or not?
22.18: Jesus saw through their trap: You hypocrites—why are you testing me?
22.19: Show me the coin used for the tax. They handed him a denarius.
22.20: He asked: Whose image and inscription is this?
22.21: Caesar's, they said. Then he replied: Give to Caesar what belongs to Caesar—and give to God what belongs to God.
22.22: They were astonished. They left him and went away.
22.23: That same day the Sadducees—who deny the resurrection—came to him with a question.
22.24: Teacher, Moses said that if a man dies without children, his brother must marry the widow and produce heirs for him.
22.25: Among us there were seven brothers. The first married and then died childless, leaving his wife to his brother.
22.26: The same happened with the second and third, down to the seventh.
22.28: So in the resurrection—whose wife will she be? All seven were married to her.
22.29: Jesus replied: You're wrong—because you don't understand Scripture or the power of God.
22.30: At the resurrection, people don't marry or give in marriage—they're like the angels in heaven.
22.31: As for the resurrection of the dead—haven't you read what God himself said:
22.32: I am the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, the God of Jacob. He is not the God of the dead but of the living.
22.33: When the crowds heard this, they were astonished at his teaching.
22.34: When the Pharisees heard that Jesus had silenced the Sadducees, they regrouped.
22.35: One of them, a legal expert, tried to trap him:
22.36: Teacher, which commandment in the Law is the greatest?
22.37: Jesus replied: Love the Lord your God with your whole heart, your whole soul, and your whole mind.
22.38: This is the first and greatest commandment.
22.39: And the second is just like it: Love your neighbor as yourself.
22.40: Every law and every prophet hangs on these two commandments.
22.41: While the Pharisees were still gathered, Jesus put a question to them:
22.42: What do you think about the Messiah—whose son is he? The son of David, they replied.
22.43: Then how, Jesus asked, does David, speaking by the Spirit, call him Lord? He says:
22.44: The Lord said to my Lord: Sit at my right hand until I place your enemies as a footstool under your feet.
22.45: If David calls him Lord, how can he be David's son?
22.46: No one could answer him a word, and from that day on no one dared question him further.

23.1: Jesus then addressed the crowds and his disciples.
23.2: The religious scholars and Pharisees occupy Moses' seat of authority.
23.3: So follow what they teach, but don't imitate what they do—because they say one thing and do another.
23.4: They pile heavy burdens on people's shoulders but won't lift a finger to help carry them.
23.5: Everything they do is a performance for an audience. They make their prayer boxes wide and their tassels long.
23.6: They love the best seats at banquets and the front seats in synagogues.
23.7: They love being greeted with respect in public and being called Rabbi.
23.8: But you are not to be called Rabbi—you have one Teacher, and you are all on equal footing as brothers and sisters.
23.9: And don't call anyone on earth Father as a title of spiritual authority—you have one Father, and he is in heaven.
23.10: And don't be called Leaders—you have one Leader, the Messiah.
23.11: The greatest among you will be the one who serves.
23.12: Those who promote themselves will be brought low; those who make themselves low will be lifted up.
23.13: How terrible for you, religious scholars and Pharisees—you pretenders! You slam the door of God's kingdom in people's faces. You don't enter yourselves, and you block those who are trying to enter.
23.14: How terrible for you, religious scholars and Pharisees—you pretenders! You exploit widows and offer long prayers as a cover. Your judgment will be all the heavier.
23.15: How terrible for you, religious scholars and Pharisees—you pretenders! You travel sea and land to win a single convert, and then make them twice as fit for Gehenna as you are.
23.16: How terrible for you, blind guides! You say: Swearing by the temple means nothing, but swearing by the temple's gold is binding.
23.17: You blind fools! Which is greater—the gold, or the temple that makes the gold sacred?
23.18: You also say: Swearing by the altar means nothing, but swearing by the gift on it is binding.
23.19: You blind men! Which is greater—the gift, or the altar that makes the gift sacred?
23.20: So whoever swears by the altar swears by it and everything on it.
23.21: Whoever swears by the temple swears by it and by the One who dwells in it.
23.22: And whoever swears by heaven swears by God's throne and the One who sits on it.
23.23: How terrible for you, religious scholars and Pharisees—you pretenders! You carefully tithe your herbs—mint, dill, and cumin—while neglecting the weightier matters of the Law: justice, compassion, and faithfulness. You should have practiced these without neglecting the others.
23.24: Blind guides! You strain out a gnat from your cup but swallow a whole camel.
23.25: How terrible for you, religious scholars and Pharisees—you pretenders! You polish the outside of the cup and plate, while the inside is filled with greed and self-indulgence.
23.26: Blind Pharisee! Clean the inside of the cup first—then the outside will be clean too.
23.27: How terrible for you, religious scholars and Pharisees—you pretenders! You're like freshly whitewashed tombs—beautiful on the outside but full of dead bones and rottenness within.
23.28: In the same way, you appear righteous to everyone—but inside you are full of hypocrisy and lawlessness.
23.29: How terrible for you, religious scholars and Pharisees—you pretenders! You build elaborate memorials for the prophets and decorate the tombs of the righteous.
23.30: And you say: If we had lived in our ancestors' time, we would never have joined in killing the prophets.
23.31: In saying that, you testify against yourselves—you are descendants of those who murdered the prophets.
23.32: Go ahead—finish what your ancestors started!
23.33: You snakes—snake-spawn! How will you escape Gehenna's judgment?
23.34: I am sending you prophets, sages, and scholars. You will kill and crucify some of them; you will flog others in your synagogues and hound them from town to town.
23.35: The result will be that all the righteous blood shed on earth will fall on you—from the blood of Abel down to Zechariah son of Berechiah, whom you murdered between the sanctuary and the altar.
23.36: I tell you truly—all of this will fall on this generation.
23.37: Jerusalem, Jerusalem—you who kill the prophets and stone those God sends to you! How many times I have wanted to gather your children together the way a hen gathers her chicks under her wings—but you refused.
23.38: Look—your house is being left to you desolate.
23.39: For I tell you—you won't see me again until you say: Blessed is the one who comes in the name of the Lord.

24.1: Jesus left the temple and was walking away when his disciples came to point out the impressive temple buildings.
24.2: He replied: Do you see all this? I tell you truly—not one stone here will be left on another; every single one will be thrown down.
24.3: As he sat on the Mount of Olives, the disciples came to him privately: Tell us—when will all this happen? And what will signal your coming and the end of the age?
24.4: Jesus replied: Be on guard—don't let anyone deceive you.
24.5: Many will come in my name claiming to be the Messiah, and they will mislead many people.
24.6: You'll hear about wars and rumors of wars—don't be alarmed. These things must happen, but they don't mean the end has arrived.
24.7: Nations and kingdoms will rise against each other. There will be famines and earthquakes in various places.
24.8: But all of this is just the beginning of the birth pains.
24.9: Then they will hand you over to be persecuted and killed, and you will be hated by all nations because of me.
24.10: At that time many will fall away from the faith, betraying and hating one another.
24.11: Many false prophets will rise up and mislead many.
24.12: Because lawlessness will multiply, the love of most people will grow cold.
24.13: But whoever endures to the end will be saved.
24.14: And this good news of the kingdom will be proclaimed throughout the entire world as a witness to all nations—and then the end will come.
24.15: So when you see what Daniel the prophet called the abomination that causes desolation, standing in the holy place—reader, pay careful attention—
24.16: then those in Judea must flee to the mountains.
24.17: Don't stop to take anything from your house.
24.18: If you're in a field, don't go back for your cloak.
24.19: How terrible it will be for pregnant women and nursing mothers in those days!
24.20: Pray that your escape won't happen in winter or on the Sabbath.
24.21: Because at that time there will be suffering greater than anything that has ever happened since the world began—or ever will happen again.
24.22: If those days hadn't been cut short, no living person would survive. But for the sake of God's chosen people, those days will be shortened.
24.23: At that time, if anyone says to you: Look, the Messiah is here! or There he is!—don't believe it.
24.24: False messiahs and false prophets will appear and perform impressive signs and wonders, enough to mislead even God's chosen people if that were possible.
24.25: I've warned you ahead of time.
24.26: So if someone says: He's out in the desert!—don't go. Or: He's hiding in a secret room!—don't believe it.
24.27: Because the coming of the Son of Man will be like lightning—visible from east to west across the whole sky.
24.28: Wherever there's a carcass, the vultures will gather.
24.29: Immediately after the suffering of those days, the sun will go dark, the moon will stop shining, the stars will fall from the sky, and the powers of heaven will be shaken.
24.30: Then the sign of the Son of Man will appear in the sky, and all the nations of the earth will mourn as they see the Son of Man coming on the clouds of heaven with power and immense glory.
24.31: He will send out his angels with the great blast of a trumpet, and they will gather his chosen people from the four winds—from one end of heaven to the other.
24.32: Learn from the fig tree: when its branches become tender and its leaves come out, you know summer is close.
24.33: In the same way, when you see all these things happening, know that it is near—right at the door.
24.34: I tell you truly—this generation will certainly not pass away before all these things happen.
24.35: Heaven and earth will pass away—but my words will never pass away.
24.36: But no one knows the day or hour—not the angels in heaven, not even the Son—only the Father.
24.37: The coming of the Son of Man will be just like the days of Noah.
24.38: Before the flood, people were eating and drinking, marrying and being married, right up until the day Noah entered the ark.
24.39: They had no idea what was coming until the flood swept them all away. That's exactly how the coming of the Son of Man will be.
24.40: Two men will be in a field—one taken, the other left.
24.41: Two women grinding grain—one taken, the other left.
24.42: So stay awake—because you don't know what day your Lord is coming.
24.43: Understand this: if a homeowner had known when a thief was coming, he would have stayed awake and not let his house be broken into.
24.44: So you must also be ready—the Son of Man will come at a moment when you least expect it.
24.45: Who is the faithful and wise servant that the master has put in charge of his household staff to give them their food at the right time?
24.46: How favored that servant will be when the master comes and finds him doing his job.
24.47: I tell you, the master will put him in charge of everything he owns.
24.48: But if that servant turns wicked and thinks: My master is taking his time—
24.49: and starts beating his fellow servants and carousing with drunkards—
24.50: that servant's master will come on a day he doesn't expect and at an hour he doesn't know.
24.51: He will cut him to pieces and assign him a place with the pretenders—where there is weeping and grinding of teeth.

25.1: The kingdom of heaven will be like ten young women who took their lamps and went to wait for the groom.
25.2: Five were foolish and five were wise.
25.3: The foolish ones took their lamps but brought no extra oil.
25.4: The wise ones brought extra oil in jars along with their lamps.
25.5: The bridegroom was delayed, and they all dozed off and fell asleep.
25.6: At midnight a shout rang out: The groom is here! Come out to meet him!
25.7: All ten woke up and trimmed their lamps.
25.8: The foolish ones said to the wise: Give us some of your oil—our lamps are dying out.
25.9: No, the wise ones said—there won't be enough for all of us. Go buy your own.
25.10: While they were gone to buy oil, the groom arrived. The ones who were ready went in with him to the banquet—and the door was shut.
25.11: Later the others arrived: Lord, Lord, let us in!
25.12: But he replied: I tell you truthfully—I don't know you.
25.13: So stay awake—you don't know the day or the hour.
25.14: It's also like a man who was going on a long journey and called his servants together to entrust his assets to them.
25.15: To one he gave five talents, to another two, to another one—each according to their ability. Then he left.
25.16: The one who received five talents went right to work and earned five more.
25.17: The one with two did the same and gained two more.
25.18: But the one who received one talent dug a hole and buried his master's money.
25.19: After a long time the master returned and settled accounts with them.
25.20: The one with five talents came: Master, you entrusted five talents to me—look, I've gained five more.
25.21: His master said: Well done, good and faithful servant! You were trustworthy with a small amount—I'll put you in charge of much more. Come and share in your master's joy!
25.22: The one with two talents came: Master, you gave me two—I've earned two more.
25.23: Well done, good and faithful servant! You were trustworthy with a little—I'll put you in charge of much more. Come and share your master's joy!
25.24: Then the one with one talent came: Master, I knew you were a hard man—reaping where you didn't sow, gathering where you didn't scatter.
25.25: I was afraid, so I buried it. Here—it's yours back.
25.26: His master replied: You wicked, lazy servant! You knew I reap where I haven't sown?
25.27: Then you should have at least deposited it with a bank so I'd get it back with interest.
25.28: Take the talent from him and give it to the one who has ten.
25.29: Those who produce will be given more and have plenty. From those who produce nothing, even what little they have will be taken.
25.30: Throw the useless servant into the outer darkness—where there is weeping and grinding of teeth.
25.31: When the Son of Man comes in his glory with all the angels, he will sit on his glorious throne.
25.32: All the nations will be gathered before him and he will separate people from one another as a shepherd separates sheep from goats.
25.33: The sheep he'll place on his right, the goats on his left.
25.34: Then the King will say to those on his right: Come, you who are blessed by my Father—receive the kingdom prepared for you since the foundation of the world.
25.35: Because I was hungry and you fed me; I was thirsty and you gave me something to drink; I was a stranger and you welcomed me;
25.36: I had no clothing and you clothed me; I was sick and you cared for me; I was in prison and you visited me.
25.37: Then the righteous will ask: Lord, when did we see you hungry and feed you, or thirsty and give you a drink?
25.38: When did we see you as a stranger and welcome you, or needing clothes and dress you?
25.39: When did we see you sick or in prison and visit you?
25.40: The King will reply: I tell you truly—whatever you did for even the least of my brothers and sisters, you did for me.
25.41: Then he will say to those on his left: Get away from me, you who are cursed—into the eternal fire prepared for the devil and his angels.
25.42: Because I was hungry and you gave me nothing to eat; I was thirsty and you gave me nothing to drink;
25.43: I was a stranger and you didn't welcome me; I had no clothes and you didn't clothe me; I was sick and in prison and you didn't visit me.
25.44: Then they will say: Lord, when did we see you hungry or thirsty or a stranger or needing clothes or sick or in prison—and fail to help you?
25.45: He will reply: I tell you truly—whatever you failed to do for even the least of my brothers and sisters, you failed to do for me.
25.46: Then these will go away into eternal punishment, and the righteous into the life of the age to come.

26.1: When Jesus had finished all these teachings, he said to his disciples:
26.2: You know that the Passover is two days away—and the Son of Man will be handed over to be crucified.
26.3: At that same time the chief priests and elders gathered at the palace of Caiaphas the high priest.
26.4: They conspired to arrest Jesus secretly and have him killed.
26.5: But not during the festival, they said—we don't want a riot among the people.
26.6: While Jesus was in Bethany at the home of Simon the Leper,
26.7: a woman came to him with an alabaster jar of very expensive perfume and poured it over his head while he was at the table.
26.8: The disciples were angry: What a waste!
26.9: This could have been sold for a large sum and given to the poor.
26.10: Jesus overheard them: Why are you troubling this woman? She has done something beautiful for me.
26.11: The poor will always be with you—but I won't always be here.
26.12: By pouring this perfume on me, she prepared my body for burial.
26.13: I tell you truly—wherever this good news is proclaimed in all the world, what she has done will be told as well, in her memory.
26.14: Then Judas Iscariot, one of the twelve, went to the chief priests
26.15: and asked: What will you pay me if I hand him over to you? They counted out thirty pieces of silver.
26.16: From that moment Judas was looking for the right opportunity to betray him.
26.17: On the first day of the Festival of Unleavened Bread, the disciples came to Jesus: Where do you want us to prepare the Passover meal?
26.18: He replied: Go into the city to a certain person and say: The Teacher says his appointed time is near—he will observe the Passover at your house with his disciples.
26.19: The disciples did as Jesus had directed and prepared the Passover meal.
26.20: When evening came, Jesus was reclining at the table with the twelve.
26.21: During the meal he said: I tell you truly—one of you will betray me.
26.22: They were deeply grieved and began asking one by one: Surely not I, Lord?
26.23: He replied: The one who dipped his hand in the bowl with me—he is the one.
26.24: The Son of Man goes just as Scripture has written of him. But how terrible for the man who betrays him—it would have been better for that man if he had never been born.
26.25: Then Judas, who was about to betray him, said: Surely not I, Rabbi? Jesus replied: You said it.
26.26: While they were eating, Jesus took bread, gave thanks, broke it and gave it to his disciples: Take and eat—this is my body.
26.27: Then he took a cup, gave thanks, and gave it to them: All of you drink from it.
26.28: This is my blood of the covenant—poured out for many for the forgiveness of sins.
26.29: I tell you—I will not drink this fruit of the vine again until that day when I drink it new with you in my Father's kingdom.
26.30: After singing a hymn, they went out to the Mount of Olives.
26.31: Then Jesus said: This very night every one of you will stumble because of me—for Scripture says: I will strike the shepherd and the sheep of the flock will be scattered.
26.32: But after I am raised, I will go ahead of you to Galilee.
26.33: Peter said: Even if everyone else stumbles because of you, I never will.
26.34: I tell you truly, Jesus replied—this very night, before the rooster crows, you will deny me three times.
26.35: Peter insisted: Even if I have to die with you, I will never deny you. All the other disciples said the same.
26.36: Then Jesus went with them to a place called Gethsemane and said: Sit here while I go over there to pray.
26.37: He took Peter and the two sons of Zebedee with him and began to be overwhelmed with sorrow and anguish.
26.38: He said: My soul is crushed with grief to the point of death. Stay here and keep watch with me.
26.39: He went a little further, fell face-down, and prayed: My Father, if it is possible, let this cup pass from me. Yet not as I will—only as you will.
26.40: He returned to the disciples and found them asleep. He said to Peter: So you couldn't stay awake with me for even one hour?
26.41: Stay awake and pray so you don't fall into temptation. The spirit is eager but the body is weak.
26.42: He went away a second time and prayed: My Father, if this cup cannot pass unless I drink it, then your will be done.
26.43: He came back again and found them asleep—their eyes were heavy with exhaustion.
26.44: He left them again and prayed a third time, saying the same words.
26.45: Then he came back to the disciples: Still sleeping? Still resting? Look—the hour has arrived. The Son of Man is being handed over to sinners.
26.46: Get up—let's go. My betrayer is here.
26.47: While he was still speaking, Judas—one of the twelve—arrived with a large armed crowd sent by the chief priests and elders.
26.48: The betrayer had arranged a signal: The man I kiss is the one—arrest him.
26.49: He went straight to Jesus: Greetings, Rabbi! And he kissed him.
26.50: Jesus said: Friend—do what you came to do. Then they stepped forward, grabbed Jesus, and arrested him.
26.51: Suddenly one of those with Jesus drew his sword and struck the high priest's servant, cutting off his ear.
26.52: Jesus said: Put your sword away. Everyone who takes up the sword will die by the sword.
26.53: Do you think I couldn't call on my Father right now and he would immediately send more than twelve legions of angels?
26.54: But then how would the Scriptures be fulfilled that say this must happen?
26.55: At that moment Jesus said to the crowd: Am I some kind of armed rebel, that you've come out with swords and clubs to take me? Every day I sat teaching in the temple and you didn't arrest me.
26.56: But all this has happened to fulfil what the prophets wrote. Then all the disciples deserted him and ran.
26.57: Those who had arrested Jesus took him to Caiaphas the high priest, where the religious scholars and elders had gathered.
26.58: Peter followed at a distance as far as the high priest's courtyard. He went inside and sat with the guards to see how it would end.
26.59: The chief priests and the entire council were looking for false testimony against Jesus to have him executed.
26.60: But they couldn't find anything, though many false witnesses came forward. Finally two came forward
26.61: and said: This man claimed, I can destroy God's temple and rebuild it in three days.
26.62: The high priest stood up: Don't you have an answer? What about this testimony against you?
26.63: Jesus remained silent. The high priest said: I put you under oath by the living God—tell us whether you are the Messiah, the Son of God.
26.64: Jesus replied: You said it. But I tell you all—from now on you will see the Son of Man seated at the right hand of the Almighty, coming on the clouds of heaven.
26.65: The high priest tore his robes: He has blasphemed! Why do we need more witnesses? You've heard the blasphemy yourselves!
26.66: What is your verdict? He deserves death, they answered.
26.67: Then they spit in his face and beat him with their fists. Others slapped him,
26.68: saying: Prophesy to us, Messiah—who hit you?
26.69: Meanwhile Peter was sitting in the courtyard. A servant girl came to him: You were with Jesus of Galilee too.
26.70: He denied it in front of everyone: I don't know what you're talking about.
26.71: He went out to the gateway, where another servant girl spotted him and said to the people: This man was with Jesus of Nazareth.
26.72: He denied it again—this time with an oath: I don't know the man!
26.73: A little while later, those standing nearby said: You really are one of them—your accent gives you away.
26.74: Then he began to curse and swear: I don't know the man! And at that moment the rooster crowed.
26.75: Then Peter remembered what Jesus had said: Before the rooster crows, you will deny me three times. He went outside and wept with bitter grief.

27.1: At dawn all the chief priests and elders of the people made their final plan to execute Jesus.
27.2: They bound him, led him away, and handed him over to Pilate the governor.
27.3: When Judas, who had betrayed him, saw that Jesus had been condemned, he was seized with remorse. He brought back the thirty pieces of silver to the chief priests and elders.
27.4: I have sinned, he said. I betrayed innocent blood. What's that to us? they replied. That's your problem.
27.5: Judas threw the money into the temple and left. Then he went out and hanged himself.
27.6: The chief priests picked up the coins: It's not legal to put this in the temple treasury—it's blood money.
27.7: After deliberating, they used the money to buy a potter's field as a burial place for foreigners.
27.8: That's why that field has been called the Field of Blood to this day.
27.9: This fulfilled what Jeremiah the prophet had written: They took the thirty pieces of silver—the price the people of Israel had set on him—
27.10: and bought the potter's field with them, as the Lord directed me.
27.11: Jesus stood before the governor. The governor asked: Are you the King of the Jews? You say so, Jesus replied.
27.12: When the chief priests and elders accused him, he said nothing.
27.13: Pilate asked: Don't you hear the charges they're making against you?
27.14: Jesus gave no reply—not a single word. The governor was astonished.
27.15: At festival time, the governor had a custom of releasing a prisoner chosen by the crowd.
27.16: At that time a notorious prisoner named Barabbas was being held.
27.17: So when the crowd had gathered, Pilate asked: Which one do you want me to release—Barabbas or Jesus who is called the Messiah?
27.18: He knew they had handed Jesus over out of jealousy.
27.19: While Pilate was seated at the judgment seat, his wife sent him a message: Have nothing to do with that innocent man—I suffered greatly in a dream about him today.
27.20: But the chief priests and elders had already worked the crowd—persuading them to demand Barabbas and have Jesus executed.
27.21: The governor asked: Which of the two do you want released? Barabbas! they shouted.
27.22: Pilate asked: Then what should I do with Jesus who is called the Messiah? Crucify him! they all cried.
27.23: What crime has he committed? Pilate asked. But they only screamed louder: Crucify him!
27.24: When Pilate saw he was getting nowhere—only stirring up more uproar—he took water and washed his hands in front of the crowd: I am innocent of this man's blood. That's your responsibility.
27.25: All the people answered: His blood be on us and on our children.
27.26: Then he released Barabbas. But he had Jesus flogged and handed him over to be crucified.
27.27: The governor's soldiers took Jesus into the Praetorium and assembled the entire cohort around him.
27.28: They stripped off his clothes and put a scarlet robe on him.
27.29: They wove a crown of thorns and jammed it on his head, put a reed in his right hand, and knelt mockingly before him: Hail, King of the Jews!
27.30: They spat on him and beat him on the head with the reed.
27.31: After mocking him, they took off the robe, dressed him in his own clothes, and led him away to be crucified.
27.32: On the way out they came across a man named Simon from Cyrene and forced him to carry the cross.
27.33: They came to a place called Golgotha—the Place of the Skull.
27.34: They offered him wine mixed with gall. He tasted it but refused to drink.
27.35: After crucifying him, they divided his clothing among themselves by casting lots.
27.36: Then they sat down and kept watch over him.
27.37: Above his head they posted the written charge against him: THIS IS JESUS, THE KING OF THE JEWS.
27.38: Two criminals were crucified alongside him—one on his right, one on his left.
27.39: People passing by were hurling abuse at him, shaking their heads:
27.40: You claimed you could destroy the temple and rebuild it in three days—save yourself! Come down from the cross if you're the Son of God!
27.41: In the same way the chief priests, religious scholars, and elders mocked him:
27.42: He saved others—can't he save himself? He's the King of Israel! Let him come down from the cross right now, and we'll believe in him.
27.43: He trusted in God—let God rescue him now, if he wants him. He said he was the Son of God.
27.44: The criminals crucified alongside him were heaping the same abuse on him.
27.45: From noon until three in the afternoon, darkness covered the whole land.
27.46: Around three o'clock Jesus cried out with a loud voice: Eli, Eli, lema sabachthani?—which means: My God, my God, why have you abandoned me?
27.47: Some of those standing there heard and said: He's calling for Elijah.
27.48: One of them ran immediately, soaked a sponge in wine vinegar, put it on a reed, and offered it to him to drink.
27.49: The others said: Leave him alone—let's see if Elijah comes to save him.
27.50: Jesus cried out again with a loud voice—and released his spirit.
27.51: At that moment the temple curtain was torn in two from top to bottom. The earth shook and rocks split.
27.52: Tombs split open and many holy people who had died were raised to life.
27.53: They came out of the tombs after Jesus' resurrection and appeared to many people in the holy city.
27.54: When the centurion and those guarding Jesus saw the earthquake and everything that happened, they were terrified: This man really was the Son of God!
27.55: Many women were watching from a distance—women who had followed Jesus from Galilee and served him.
27.56: Among them were Mary Magdalene, Mary the mother of James and Joseph, and the mother of Zebedee's sons.
27.57: As evening came, a wealthy man from Arimathea named Joseph arrived—he had also become a disciple of Jesus.
27.58: He went to Pilate and requested the body of Jesus. Pilate ordered that it be released to him.
27.59: Joseph took the body, wrapped it in clean linen cloth,
27.60: and placed it in his own new tomb carved out of rock. He rolled a large stone in front of the entrance and went away.
27.61: Mary Magdalene and the other Mary were sitting opposite the tomb, watching.
27.62: The next day—the day after Preparation Day—the chief priests and Pharisees went to Pilate.
27.63: Sir, we remember that deceiver claimed while he was still alive: After three days I will rise.
27.64: So order the tomb secured until the third day. Otherwise his disciples might steal the body and tell the people he rose from the dead. That deception would be worse than the first.
27.65: Pilate said: Take a guard detachment. Go make it as secure as you can.
27.66: They went and sealed the stone and posted guards at the tomb.

28.1: After the Sabbath, as the first day of the week was dawning, Mary Magdalene and the other Mary went to see the tomb.
28.2: Suddenly there was a great earthquake—an angel of the Lord descended from heaven, rolled back the stone, and sat on it.
28.3: His appearance blazed like lightning, and his clothing was white as snow.
28.4: The guards were so terrified that they shook and became like corpses.
28.5: The angel said to the women: Don't be afraid. I know you're looking for Jesus who was crucified.
28.6: He is not here—he has risen, exactly as he said he would. Come and see where he was lying.
28.7: Now go quickly and tell his disciples: He has risen from the dead—and he is going ahead of you to Galilee, where you will see him. This is the message I was sent to deliver.
28.8: The women left the tomb quickly—frightened but overflowing with joy—and ran to tell his disciples.
28.9: Suddenly Jesus appeared and met them: Greetings! They came to him, grasped his feet, and worshipped him.
28.10: Jesus said: Don't be afraid. Go tell my brothers to go to Galilee—they will see me there.
28.11: While the women were on their way, some of the guards went into the city and reported everything that had happened to the chief priests.
28.12: The chief priests met with the elders and came up with a plan. They gave the soldiers a large sum of money
28.13: with these instructions: Tell people his disciples came at night and stole his body while you were asleep.
28.14: If the governor hears about it, we'll smooth things over with him and keep you out of trouble.
28.15: The soldiers took the money and spread the story as instructed. This account has circulated among the Jewish people to this day.
28.16: The eleven disciples went to Galilee, to the mountain Jesus had specified.
28.17: When they saw him, they worshipped—but some hesitated.
28.18: Jesus came to them and said: All authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me.
28.19: Therefore go and make disciples of all nations—baptizing them in the name of the Father and the Son and the Holy Spirit—
28.20: and teaching them to follow everything I have commanded you. And know this—I am with you every day, until the very end of the age.
